title: Fitting a twin-gang socket from a single
subtitle: Replace a single 13-amp socket with a double using the existing cable
excerpt: How to replace a single 13-amp wall socket with a twin-gang socket: isolating the circuit, opening the back box, checking the wiring, fitting the new faceplate, and testing with a socket tester.

glossaryTerms.definition: A socket faceplate carrying two 13-amp BS 1363 outlets in a single unit, connected to the same supply cable as the original single socket. A twin-gang flush faceplate fits onto the same single back box if the box has sufficient depth; a larger back box may be required if the existing box is shallow.

The metal or plastic mounting box set into the wall that houses the wiring connections behind a socket or switch faceplate. Standard single back boxes are 25 mm or 35 mm deep; twin faceplates need at least 25 mm to accommodate the connections. Check the depth before buying the faceplate.

content.text: Replacing a single socket with a

is one of the most common DIY electrical jobs and does not require new cable. The existing ring-main or radial circuit cable connects to the new faceplate in exactly the same way as the old single. The only variable is whether the existing
is deep enough for the extra connections.

Always use the live-dead-live isolation procedure: test the socket is live, switch off the MCB and fit the lock-off device, test the socket is dead, proceed. A lock-off alone is not sufficient; verify dead with the two-pole tester before touching any conductor.

Test the socket is live with the two-pole tester. Switch off the MCB that controls this circuit and fit the lock-off device. Test the socket again: it must be dead before you unscrew the faceplate. If you are unsure which MCB controls the socket, switch off each in turn while testing the socket.

Unscrew and remove the faceplate. Note the cable connections: a ring-main socket will have two cables (two live, two neutral, two earth), a spur will have one. Photograph the connections before disconnecting. Measure the back box depth; if it is shallower than 25 mm, fit a deeper box before proceeding.

Connect the cable(s) to the new twin faceplate terminals: brown (live) to L, blue (neutral) to N, green/yellow (earth) to E. On a ring-main socket, both cables connect to the same terminals; the faceplate terminals are designed to take two wires. Trim and re-strip any wire ends that were scored or nicked when removing them; use the wire strippers to produce a clean 8 mm bare conductor.

Fold the cable neatly into the back box and screw the faceplate home. Remove the lock-off device and switch the MCB back on. Plug the socket tester into each outlet; all three LEDs should indicate correct wiring (live/neutral/earth present and in the correct terminals). Test the RCD trip function if the socket tester has a trip button.

attrs.symptom: Socket tester shows wiring fault after fitting
attrs.cause: A conductor is in the wrong terminal, or a wire is not fully inserted into the terminal.
attrs.fix: Isolate again, remove the faceplate, and check each connection against the terminal markings (L, N, E).

attrs.cause: A live conductor is touching the back box (earthed), or a neutral and earth are reversed.
attrs.fix: Isolate, recheck all connections with the photograph taken during dismantling. Confirm no bare copper touches the metal back box.
